editorialSummary.text: Classic burger chain serving customizable burgers, hand-cut fries & shakes.

text.text:
Visited December 16, 2025

Five stars without hesitation. We are heading out tomorrow on a 17 day cruise to Hawaii and flew into San Francisco a day early to settle in and enjoy the city. My original plan for lunch was Chinese food, but I opened my big mouth and told my kids a story from 2020. When we were in LA on our way to Hawaii back then, we stopped at In N Out. My husband and I thought our older daughter, who was not even two at the time, would share the burger with us. Instead she ate the entire thing herself and my husband had to go get another one. 😆

That story completely sealed our fate. My older daughter refused anything but In N Out, so from the Marriott Marquis where we are staying, we hopped on the cable car and rode all the way there. The place was absolutely packed, but the energy was great and the line moved efficiently. Despite how busy it was, the staff remained friendly, calm, and welcoming, and we were lucky enough to manage getting a table.

A special shout out to Dino who was bussing tables. He was incredibly attentive and kind. He noticed our two little girls right away and surprised them with kids activity books, which completely made their day. He checked on us several times, brought extra napkins, and made sure we were comfortable the entire time. That level of genuine care really stood out.

For the girls, we ordered two Double Doubles with just cheese, cheese fries, and chocolate milkshakes. For my husband and me, we ordered two Double Doubles with added chilies and grilled onions along with Animal Fries. We made our lemon up ourselves and it was so fresh and so good. Every single bite was delicious and absolutely worth the trip. The food was hot, fresh, and exactly as remembered, maybe even better.

After eating, we took the cable car straight back to the hotel and everyone promptly passed out. Waking up at 1 am was no fun, but this experience was completely worth it and gave us such a fun family memory right before our cruise.

The only small hiccup was entirely on us. We forgot hand sanitizer and the restaurant did not have any available. My six year old had basically touched everything on the cable car 😆 so my husband walked across the street to buy some and paid six dollars for a tiny bottle that would normally cost a dollar elsewhere. Oh well 😆😆

Overall, the food, the service, and the kindness of the staff made this an easy five star experience. Thank you to Dino and the entire team for such a memorable and welcoming visit. We loved every single moment and every bite.

text.text: Stopped by the In-N-Out at Pier 39 while visiting San Francisco. It was pretty packed, but the line moved along fairly quickly considering how busy it was. Took a little while to get my food, but honestly not bad for how many people were there. The food was, as always, solid—classic In-N-Out quality. Finding a seat took a bit, but once I did, I enjoyed my meal. Overall, a good experience and I’d definitely come back next time I’m in the area.

text.text: Loved my first ever visit, specifically at their Fisherman's Wharf branch in San Francisco, CA. Although their menu was not very comprehensive, the food served were great-tasting and exceeded my expectations. The service crew were also very pleasant. One thing to note, though, is that it can be very packed at certain hours of the day and may be difficult to find seats. Overall, I highly recommend this place to those craving burgers and fries, and milkshake on the side.

text.text:
It seems that in the great state of California, one cannot speak of cuisine without mentioning a fabled establishment In-N-Out Burger. My partner insisted it was an experience one must have, a culinary rite of passage, a sacred tradition.

So, on a lazy afternoon, we decided to indulge this local enthusiasm. The place positively brimming with life, a bustling crowd united by their devotion to burgers and the faint scent of grilled promise. Clearly, this was no ordinary eatery; it was a cult favourite.

I wouldn’t dare compare it to the gourmet creations of fine restaurants, where one must decipher menus and pay for artistic interpretation and to be honest that wouldn’t be a fair at all. For fast food, it was most commendable. The flavor was honest, the texture agreeable and it does sit in the upper level of fast food burger hierarchy.

It was a charmingly sincere experience and I see the point my partner was trying to make.

text.text: It’s always been my dream to stay here and we were finally able to make it come true this December! My sister and I were attending an event at the Masonic which is walking distance from the Fairmont. With the combination of that and it being decorated for the holidays it was a no brainer and we knew we had to stay here! The lobby is so magical during the holidays with the decorations and huge gingerbread house. Our room was very nice and had comfy beds. All of the hotel staff was exceptionally kind and very helpful. I would definitely stay here again! They also are home to the Tonga room restaurant and if you have time I HIGHLY recommend you go!

text.text:
I stayed at the Fairmont San Francisco during a recent trip, and it really does live up to its reputation. The hotel itself feels like a historic landmark, and walking into the lobby is almost like stepping into another era. Everything is polished, elegant, and very well kept. The staff greeted me right away, helped with bags, and made the check in process simple. It was clear they took pride in taking care of people.

My room had a great view over the city, and the space felt comfortable and quiet. The bed was probably one of the best I have slept in during a hotel stay, and the bathroom had plenty of counter space and good lighting. Housekeeping kept everything spotless the entire time, and every request I made was handled quickly. Even something small like asking for extra towels felt like it mattered to them.

The location on Nob Hill is also perfect if you want to explore. You can walk to a lot of places if you do not mind the hills, and it is easy to grab a cable car or rideshare when you want to get across town. I enjoyed the restaurants inside the hotel as well, especially for breakfast. The Fairmont is not the cheapest option in the city, but for a special trip or a visit where you want everything to feel a little elevated, it is definitely worth it. I would stay here again without hesitation.

text.text:
Travelled from Melbourne , Australia to stay here for 2 nights 12th and 13th December. The hotel itself is visibly stunning! It felt so festive & lovely when we walked in. Unfortunately due to the level of popularity the Christmas display attracts - it’s makes it very difficult for hotel guests to walk around without disrupting photos being taken & bumping into families with children. Would recommend having select times for access.

Check in process - not great - although the staff member was delightful, we were given poor directions resulting in quite a delay getting into our room when it was late.

The room was lovely looking HOWEVER noticed the bathroom sink had green mildew in it. (The hole at the front). The hairdryer - likely from when the hotel was first constructed - for such a lovely hotel, would strongly recommend upgrading these for female guests - not to mention it being a fire hazard with so much dust built up on the filter.  TV - very small for such a large room and difficult to see when you are in bed.
Beds were very comfortable, lovely linen and toiletries were beautiful.

We were really looking forward to enjoying a drink in the palm court restaurant , however after purchasing -was told we were unable to sit on the tables as they were being set up for “a full lunch service”. This left hotel guests no where to sit. Disappointing for paying guests, unable to use facilities freely due to it “being a popular time of year “.

text.text:
The building is gorgeous, and the history displayed on the hallway walls is tremendous.

Lobby interior is ornate and striking. Check-in experience is totally chaotic, especially if you are arriving at the usual 2-6pm window. No clear lines for queuing — entitled guests “just asking a quick question” would cut the line and then delay for 10 mins while the overwhelmed front desk staff were too meek to tell them to go to the back of the line. Check in staff were hit or miss. Mine gave me ZERO information about what they were charging me, incidental costs, any amenities. Ended up with the wrong charge that I later had to come back down to correct.

Amenities — nothing is included. Wifi is extra. In 2026! Gym is extra (and outsourced to another company, so you can’t even take a towel back to your room - instead dripping sweat throughout the entire carpeted hotel as you return from your workout). They force you to sign up for their rewards program in order to gain access to amenities. Gym hours are awful (opens at 6am, it is absolutely MOBBED at that time - no treadmills or bikes open when I arrived at 6:10am). It is also on the other side of the building, so if you’re staying in the tower, it’s almost a 10 minute walk just to get there. Signs are inconclusive.

Room had a beautiful view of the bay. Old charm. But it was grubby around the edges. In need of a deep clean. Lots of dust, all of the wood materials were totally scratched and banged up. Poor outlet location for the desk. All of the upholstered fabrics were worn and not particularly clean. Cleaners themselves were punctual and thorough but clearly there needs to be more coverage and more regular deep cleans of rooms.

editorialSummary.text: Bustling Italian eatery with varied pizza options from coal-fired to Roman-style, plus beer on tap.

text.text: Tony’s Pizza Napoletana in San Francisco is a must-stop for any pizza lover. The menu has tons of styles to choose from, and every slice tastes fresh, authentic, and made with real care. The crust is perfectly cooked — crispy on the outside and soft inside — and the ingredients are high quality and flavorful. The atmosphere feels lively but still comfortable, and the staff is friendly and knowledgeable when helping you pick a pizza. Whether you’re a classic margherita fan or want to try something unique, Tony’s always delivers an amazing pizza experience. 🍕

text.text:
Delicious, authentic, romantic, and delicious!!
Went for dinner around 7:30 pm on a Thursday with my girlfriend and there was no wait for a table outside. The patio Christmas decor was so pretty and the ambiance of the place was so warm and welcoming.
We couldn’t decide between the New Yorker, the Burratina, and the Porto; all award winning pizzas.
The burratina is a thick crust pizza on the focaccia so we didn’t get that since it would’ve been a lot of food for us. We ordered the Porto. The crust was so thin and had the perfect balance between fluffy and soft. The ndjua was delicious on it but this pizza is more on the umami side of flavors. We wish we got one of the classic pizzas, but definitely coming back to try it out.
We also got the Pasta Genovesa, which we lovedddd. The pesto was super rich and every ingredient in the dish was perfectly balanced. Fresh sundried tomatoes, artichokes, pine nuts, goat cheese. Honestly would come back just for that.

We also got the Tirramisu for dessert! Absolutely must must get!

editorialSummary.text: Old-school, English-style restaurant serving acclaimed prime rib & martinis since the 1940s.

text.text: Got a last minute RSVP @10pm. Arrived early and the host graciously seated us almost half hour earlier than our reservation. My last meal here was more than 5 years ago and nothing has changed. Outstanding service, great ambiance, good foods; however, the salad was not as appetizing as before. Not sure what’s changed because it’s been so long ago (>5yrs prior). The prime rib is tender and perfectly cooked, as well as other side dishes. Overall, the meal was great. Would love to come back for special occasions.

text.text:
Great experience. Highly recommend for solo or duo travelers, not so much parties.

If no reservation, come as soon or before they open and put name on list for walk ins, or ask bartender for a bar seat (also needs wait but these turn faster for smaller parties)

Just choose the size of the prime rib you want, and yes if you finish the King Henry you get a second, smaller, slice (don’t be a dick and try to take it togo, they also won’t let you)

There is also a triple King, which is exactly as it sounds

Finish with a dessert, they’re good!

text.text:
The atmosphere is killer. Loved how it looks inside with the huge trees, lights, and multiple floors. It’s almost like one of those romantic movie film sets.

I got the breakfast burrito and my wife got the eggs Benedict. Do be aware that a lot of San Francisco restaurants add on a “mandate” charge for their employees health care that the customer has to pay for instead of the company. Beyond that, the food is reasonably priced for what you get.

Honestly I’m a bit torn. I loved the food, I loved the atmosphere, but it’s self-serve in that you can’t sit still a table until you order; and even then it’s basically cafeteria style with order and pay and then get a number. No service per say. But the food is great and the atmosphere is killer. So yeah. I’d definitely purely come back!

Excellent vibe. Was here during SB weekend so it was busy.
You place your order first and then find seating. Plenty of space and you have the option of inside or outside dining.
The servers find you by the number they give you when you placed your order.
Food was excellent. The Mac & cheese was especially good.
There are several servers walking around if you want to order another beverage or need anything. As soon as you are done there's someone cleaning the table and boom...next party is sitting down.
Oh they have a bar so I'm guessing you can just get a seat there if available.
Thia place gets the Mr. National seal of approval

text.text: January 2026. The staff are friendly and efficient, especially housekeeping and room service.  Ray and David at the Front Desk elevated our stay, and the door  personnel were amiable and competent. Our junior suite was located behind the elevators (I was told on inquiry that all of the junior suites are behind the elevators), so they sung to us throughout the day and evening. We were offered a smaller room with a better view or a larger room with an extra $250/night charge.  The suite lacked a functioning minibar and the ice machine, which worked well, was on the third floor. The restaurant advertises on the website an à la carte menu for breakfast, but when we went to breakfast, we were told ONLY buffet was available for every breakfast, so we had to return to our room and get our desired choices via room service.

places.formattedAddress: 2765 Hyde St, San Francisco, CA 94109, USA

editorialSummary.text: Opened in 1916, this corner spot serves its signature Irish coffee alongside American staples.

text.text:
Alright, I had a great time here. Known this place for ages but finally went, especially for the Irish Coffee. We went around an hr after they open and waited for 10mins to get in. It packed and they have 2 sections, bar (for drinks only - no foods serve), and shares or community tables. We got the community table and we shared with another group. The service is great from the hostess to server, they did a terrific job of explaining how things work and so forth. I got the Irish coffee (which was good but I had better) and my friend got the non alcohol Irish coffee plus the claim chowder, the pastrami sandwich, and the Vista burger. Which all are delish, we ate them all while chatting with the other folks.  The decor is simple and filled with old articles and pics of the historical place so it nice. The bartender dressed in a white coat to make it more of the oldie feeling. We all really enjoyed it and left with new friends and great memories. Check out if you're in the area and the wait line can be long so come early. Plus very convenient since it right across from the cable car and the Ghirardelli square.

text.text:
Popped in here for lunch after needing a caffeine fix after an early start. It seems like this spot is busy at all times, with some people having to share tables, so just keep that in mind if you are looking for a romantic meal.

We were seated quickly and I ordered the Irish coffee (my husband  just had a beer). Being from the UK originally we are used to Irish coffees but this was a very nice version (albeit small, especially for $16). For food we ordered the lobster roll and the club sandwich, which both arrived very quickly. The lobster roll was great, with a generous portion of lobster. My husbands club sandwich looked good as well, and he said it was very tasty. His food did come with fries, which weren't mentioned on the menu or asked for, which he was ultimately charged for. It wasn't a huge amount (something like $1.50) and I would like to think it was just a mistake, but something to be aware of.

We finished our food and our server recommended a Mexican coffee, so I had to give that a try. I actually think I preferred the Mexican coffee to the Irish coffee, so if you are a Kailua fan I recommend giving it a try). In total our bill came to $130 (including tax and tip) so is a little expensive for a lunch spot. The food is good though and I would recommend if you are in the area.

text.text:
If you’re in San Francisco and love a good cocktail experience, The Buena Vista is an absolute must-see - especially for anyone craving an iconic Irish Coffee. This legendary spot has perfected the art of the drink, and watching the bartenders line up glasses and craft each Irish Coffee with precision is truly mesmerizing. Make sure to grab a spot near the bar so you can enjoy the show.

That said, don’t expect a quiet, cozy atmosphere. The Buena Vista is lively and often packed, especially on weekends and holidays. Be prepared to wait a while for a table or even a spot at the bar during peak times. The energy and buzz are part of the charm, though—it’s all part of the experience.

Bottom line: Come for the history, stay for the Irish Coffee, and soak in the vibrant vibe of this San Francisco classic.

text.text: Had a very warm welcome by staff and was seated in the downstairs banquet area. Atmosphere is lively and the food was so fresh. We got the halibut from the daily fish special and it flaked perfectly. The risotto was surprising -- so fresh and packed with flavor! We had an amazing time and will definitely be back!

text.text: Located in the heart of Little Italy, North Beach San Francisco.  This is my favorite place for classic cioppino, fresh oysters and marinated Dungeness crab!  They offer raw oysters either on the half shell or as shooters, west coast or east coast species with a spicy red sauce.  Be sure to ask for their fresh horseradish.   For starters, we also love their bay shrimp Louie salad, which is big enough to share with 2-3 people.  I also recommend their marinaded, cold whole Dungeness crab, which comes in an olive oil, lemon garlic sauce.  Be sure to order their marinaded”Best Damn Cioppino” , which is served in a large bowl:  Dungeness crab, mussels, clams, squid, and scallops.

text.text:
We came here to try the famous Cioppino, and it absolutely lived up to its reputation. The portion is very generous — officially for two people, but honestly perfect for sharing between three. The stew was incredibly flavorful: a rich, garlicky tomato broth packed with crab and seafood. Truly delicious and a must-try.

We had the oysters as an appetizer. They were okay — fresh enough, but nothing special. Since there are so many great places in the city to eat amazing oysters, I wouldn’t necessarily recommend ordering them here.

The service was friendly and attentive. We were seated downstairs in the cellar area, which is thematically decorated and actually quite charming. The restaurant was very busy upstairs, but still well-managed.

Overall, highly recommended — especially for the Cioppino, which is absolutely worth coming here for.

text.text:
My favourite place to grab lunch at Pier 39. I love their fresh sourdough… one of the best places you can get your food from while walking on the pier.

Their clam chowder is my all time favourite and it never misses the spot. I love their waffles, and their sandwiches taste good too. My friend loves their tomato soup, I don’t like it in general.

If you don’t know what to get, I’d suggest going with their clam chowder sourdough bowl. You can dip your sourdough in the soup and enjoy every bite of it. Sourdough tastes great and the crust is perfectly done.

Finding a table can be a bit of a wait. I’d suggest a minimum company of 2 so one can find a spot to sit and the other can bring the food to the table, because it’s self serving. Plan your visit and you’ll have a great experience, especially during Christmas!

text.text: stopped in for lunch after a morning of walking along the embarcadero and visiting the sea lions at pier 39. we wanted to show the kids all the sourdough critters at boudin bakery, and opted for more comfortable seating upstairs at the bistro. it was a long weekend and the place was packed but we were promptly seated. service was polite and efficient. my daughter loved the turtle sourdough bread bowl her clam chowder came in, and didn’t have complaints for the soup either. i found the chicken, mash and carrots off the kids menu to be a great value. crab & corn bisque was ok, nothing special. the standout of our meal was definitely the dungeness crab salad. fairly generous portion of sweet, succulent crab on a bed of fresh and beautifully presented vegetables. so refreshing!

text.text:
R&G Lounge gets two stars from me because the Peking duck experience was really disappointing. For the price and the restaurant’s reputation, the way the dish came out felt careless.
What went wrong
•	The Peking duck arrived at the table with several pieces already missing, so it looked like a half-finished platter instead of a whole, impressive dish.
•	The duck was not deboned, which made it hard to eat and took away from the enjoyment, especially since Peking duck is usually served in neat, easy-to-wrap slices. Also, the duck arrived at our table as shown in the photo. There seems pieces were missing, I asked our server and was told that this is the way the duck is served. I have never been served a duck in such a way.
•	Given how well-known this place is for special dishes and banquet-style meals, the quality and presentation of the duck fell far below expectations.
Why it’s two stars, not one
•	The restaurant is a long-standing Chinatown spot with a big menu and a busy, lively dining room, so it clearly can do better than what we were served.
•	Service and other dishes may be fine for some guests, but the mishandled Peking duck turned what should have been a highlight into a letdown, and that’s hard to overlook.

text.text:
It had been about 17 years since I last ate at R&G lounge, because I was kind of boycotting due to receiving poor service so so long ago. With so many other restaurants to try, I was never interested in coming back until a friend told us that he loves R&G lounge so much that he eats here almost once a month, and convinced us all to have dinner there, after we were turned away from 4 Kings. R&G has been around all this time, and lots of people seem to love it, and of course I can forgive poor service nearly 2 decades ago.
Has R&G always been so big? They put us in large table tucked away from the commotion. They have multiple floors and even have options for private dining. I can see why large parties plan events here so they can eat the usual Chinese banquet dishes.

$65 Salt & Pepper Dungeness Crab - whole crab battered, deep fried, sprinkled w/ salt & pepper seasoning:
great! fresh & succulent fresh crab meat piled high with crispy garlic

$50 Chilean Seabass (small) - marinated and baked in a glaze:
okay! nice and tender, but due to personal preference I don't love glazed fish that much. Our other friends loved it though.

$24 Roast Squab - whole squab roasted, crispy skin, w/ seasoning salt on the side
good! crispy skin indeed and quite juicy. Are we calling MSG = seasoning salt now? It does look quite like MSG, which I have nothing against. To me it's just another form of sodium. The tough thing about squab is it is not always easy to split up. Who gets the leg, the best part?

$26 Steamed Clams & Eggs - steamed with beaten eggs:
good! steamed to a silky consistency

$20 Eggplant w/ Garlic Sauce - eggplant, black fungus, bamboo shoots & scallions sauteed in hot garlic sauce:
good! silky, spicy and flavorful

$8 for 4 bowls of steamed rice, which we shared among 6 people, because we're too cheap to get one bowl of rice per person, but we never finish our rice anyway.

3% SF Mandate is annoying, but nothing new

I guess I am due to let go of the past and admit that R&G has some pretty good dishes. In fact, service this time around was pretty good. Who could remember that nearly 20 years ago, they took nearly an hour to return our credit card, so we can just conclude payment and leave, as if they were holding us hostage? Oops, I guess I do still remember. We're moving on!

Has not been back here for over 10 years.  Did not know that they only open the basement for lunch.  I think they open second floor for dinner and main floor for bar in the late night.

Had lunch with a client.  Ordered honey spareribs, beef brisket and turnip, dried scallops with melon, and salted fish fried rice.  All the dishes were very good and full flavor.  However, one dish came short on value was the dried scallops with melon.

The portions are smaller compared to other restaurants but have full flavor.  I recommend and must try is the honey spareribs.

Arrived at 11a on a Wednesday, only two tables were occupied. Most people started to come around 12n and was a full house by 12:30.

places.formattedAddress: 672 Geary St, San Francisco, CA 94102, USA

editorialSummary.text: American spin-off of Tokyo’s standout ramen brand brings a variety of combos to petite, modern digs.

text.text:
I’ve eaten at the OG Jikasei Mensho in Tokyo, and somehow this spot in SF holds its own. The flavors genuinely compare.

The GKO Vegan is my forever order. It’s unapologetically garlicky, deeply savory, and completely umami-packed in that way that makes you pause mid-bite. It’s comforting but bold. I’ve tried to branch out, but I always come back to it.

The Vegan Tantanmen is also excellent, rich, nutty, and satisfying. The Miso is solid, but probably my least favorite of the three. Something has to be last.

There was a month when I had dinner here almost every week. No regrets.

If another ramen snob tells you there’s better ramen in SF, believe them. But also believe me. Then go eat as much ramen as you can, because honestly, why not? 🍜

text.text:
I tried the GKO and the Signature ramen at Mensho Tokyo after hearing all the hype (and knowing it’s Michelin-listed), and overall I left feeling a bit disappointed.

First off, be prepared to wait — there’s usually a long line, and the prices are definitely on the higher end. For that cost and wait time, I was expecting something exceptional.

The broths are extremely rich and very heavy. Both bowls leaned hard into bold flavors, especially garlic, which felt overpowering at times. On the positive side, they have a solid bottled beer selection and experiment with interesting and creative flavors (like matcha ramen).

Overall, I don’t quite get the level of hype. It’s good, but not worth the long wait or premium price in my opinion — especially when there’s better ramen elsewhere in the city.

text.text:
Mensho Tokyo SF is a cute little ramen spot in the Tenderloin. Went here by 5pm on a Tuesday, and was able to get easy seating for two people. Street parking was difficult to find, but thankfully there was a parking garage right next to the restaurant (Temple Garage). By the time we left by 6pm, there was a long line out the door on this rainy day. Food speaks for itself.

Ordered:
Spicy Enoki chips
Corn wings
A5 Wagyu sushi
Signature Spicy Toripaitan Ramen
Spicy Sichuan Pepper Style Vegan Miso Ramen
(With a side of chashu pork and ajitama egg)

Absolutely loved our appetizers! Was surprised that the corn was sweet, considering we’re in the middle of winter. Wagyu sushi was fun and delicious - pretty sure it was real wasabi instead of horseradish-wasabi.

Ok so for ramen -
Their signature ramen was playful, with their rich and creamy broth. This was our first time trying duck chashu. Usually not a fan of duck, but this was good! LOVEDDDD the fried burdock root in this. Will plan to order extra next time, if possible.

We also tried their Spicy Vegan Miso ramen. **disclosure: I am not a vegan.** But wanted to see this style of ramen because the reviews kept raving about how much people loved their Vegan options. && it does not disappoint! I was surprised by the delicately creamy, sesame & porcini mushroom broth. The noodles are perfectly chewy.

My only negative thing is that the Sichuan Pepper Style makes the broth taste saltier than it actually is. When I come back, will plan to order the non-spicy version. Other than that - I can see why this little hole in the wall made the Michelin list.

Will plan to come back and order:
Corn wings
Signature Spicy Toripaitan Ramen
Vegan Miso Ramen w side of ajtima egg & pork chashu

text.text: We waited about an hour on a Saturday evening, but as always, it was well worth it. The food here is fantastic—my top picks are the Lamb Ramen, Enoki Spicy Chips, and the Miso Butter Ramen. Each dish is packed with flavor and never disappoints. Also, if you enjoy sake, their Mensho sake is incredibly smooth and pairs perfectly with the meal. Definitely a spot I’ll keep coming back to!

text.text:
Mensho Tokyo SF is truly one of the standout places in the city for anyone who loves ramen 🍜. It’s not just another noodle shop — it feels genuinely unique and deeply authentic, the kind of spot where you can tell the chefs care about craft, flavor, and experience 🎌. Because of its popularity, I ended up waiting in line for a little over an hour ⏳ before getting in. Honestly, the wait itself becomes part of the ritual; everyone in line knows they’re about to enjoy something special.

Once inside, the service moves quickly ⚡. After placing my order, the food arrived much faster than I expected, especially considering how busy the restaurant was. I ordered the spicy VGKO 🌶️, the spicy enoki mushroom 🍄, and a Japanese beer 🍺 to go with it. I usually have a pretty high tolerance for spicy and hot foods, but the heat level of the spicy VGKO caught me off guard. It’s intense — delicious, but definitely not for the faint of heart 🔥. If you’re thinking of ordering the super spicy level, just be prepared.

Overall, Mensho Tokyo SF delivers a memorable ramen experience with bold flavors, efficient service, and a vibe that makes the wait worthwhile ⭐. I’d absolutely return — though next time, I might think twice before going for the highest spice level.

text.text:
We hosted our wedding dinner here, and the experience was seamless from start to finish. The coordinator and sales events team were incredibly helpful in mapping out every detail, which made the afternoon feel smooth and effortless. Upon arrival, our guests were warmly greeted and welcomed, immediately offered drinks at the bar, and guided into the dining experience. The service felt thoughtful and well-paced, allowing us to truly enjoy the moment without feeling rushed or overwhelmed.
Ava and Andrea, the event sales coordinators, provided thorough and well-designed questionnaires that captured every detail needed to properly accommodate our event. Their process made planning feel organized and stress-free, and it was clear that nothing was overlooked.

text.text:
This narrow, intimate restaurant feels like stepping into a cozy country house - dark wood, rich walls, and plush velvet seating set a dim, moody tone. The service was attentive without being intrusive, with tables cleared promptly and courses timed perfectly.

The meal started with complimentary popovers that were crisp on the outside and soft inside. Everything I tried was full of flavor. The clam chowder was rich, creamy, peppery, and loaded with clams, and the cocktail shrimp were generously sized and very fresh. I couldn't finish everything, so they thoughtfully packaged my leftovers, stored them with the hostess, and gave me a claim stub to pick them up on the way out. A great touch that kept the table uncluttered for drinks and dessert.

While the ambiance leans upscale, the overall vibe is relaxed and comfortable. I'll definitely be back to try their famous fried chicken.

text.text:
Recently, I had a dinner with my spouse at Gar Danko. The appointment was at 10. I thought they would be closing down as their shop closing time is 10. They said we are okay to eat until 12 a.m. But we had to place all our courses upfront, which is kind of not convenient.

Service-wise, it was great. All the servers were competent, helpful, and charming. They explained all the dishes and were constantly checking on us. There were multiple gift desserts to charm us.

Coming to food, the menu looked great. But beware, vegetarians, there are literally three dishes you can choose from. I would say that the place is not vegetarian-friendly. We had like 1/10 options of vegetarian and non-veg options. They offered us coffee before dessert. The latte tasted really good, and I enjoyed it a lot. To us its not worth the price we paid as its over $150 a person and its not acceptable to have poor menu options for vegetarians.

Coming to food, the menu looked great. But beware, vegetarians, there are literally three dishes you can choose from. I would say that the place is not vegetarian-friendly. We had like 1/10 options of vegetarian and non-veg options. They offered us coffee before dessert. The latte tasted really good, and I enjoyed it a lot. To us its not worth the price we paid as its over $150 a person and its not acceptable to have poor menu options for vegetarians.

text.text:
Our recent dinner at Gary Danko was unfortunately not at the level one would expect from a restaurant with such a long-standing reputation. While service began warmly and certain dishes were well prepared, the overall experience fell short in several key areas.

Food:
•	The amuse bouche tasted stale, and the sourdough was underwhelming.
•	The scallops were well-seared and enjoyable, and the duck was the strongest dish of the evening.
•	My mom had the lobster risotto, which was pretty good, but the branzino felt dry to me (though she enjoyed it).
•	The biggest disappointment was the soufflé. Mine was slightly undercooked and my mom’s was noticeably more raw in the center. When she mentioned it, our server didn’t acknowledge anything was wrong and just kept asking if she wanted something else, which felt a bit off for a restaurant of this level.
•	She switched to a crème brûlée, which was fine but nothing memorable.

After-effects:
Unfortunately, both my mom and I had stomach cramps later that night (hers started first, mine around 2am). The next day we could barely eat anything except some chicken soup with ramen noodles, which completely ruined our day.

Overall:
There were bright spots, but the inconsistency in execution, combined with the dessert mishandling and the stomach issues afterward, made the experience hard to justify at this price point. For us, it’s not a restaurant we would return to, especially with so many strong alternatives in San Francisco.

text.text:
We had a lovely anniversary dinner at Gary Danko. The service was wonderful and the team brought us some complimentary bites with happy anniversary spelled out on them. Some of the dishes such as the scallops were outstanding. Others were good but not fantastic. It seems as if they've made the decision to try to move more people through the restaurant on a nightly basis, which is probably why they can afford to serve top quality food at reasonable prices, but also probably the reason they do not have a Michelin star. The tables are very close together, in some cases requiring you to sit next to your partner instead of across the table from them. When the cheese cart is out, it is impossible to pass through the aisles near it. At our table, this resulted in many people squeezing up between our table and the cheese cart and rubbing their butt over our table as they passed. Not what you would expect in a first class restaurant. The decor also seemed a bit dark and dated to us. So not perfect, but a good experience, especially if you're a new new to fine dining and want to see what a near-Michelin experience looks like at a not-crazy expensive price. Seems like a good balance  for  trying to build a viable business in a tourist-heavy location.
The 7% health care mandate hidden in the bill is way out of line. The whole idea is ridiculous, but most restaurants in SF, with far lower average tickets, seem to get by with 3% (and plenty don't charge anything).

text.text:
Gary Danko saved our anniversary dinner.

On December 21st, San Francisco experienced a major power outage. We originally had a reservation at a different (Michelin two-star) restaurant, but they canceled on us just 40 minutes before our scheduled time due to having no power. We had traveled to SF specifically for the evening and booked a babysitter.

I called around and managed to get a 5:30 pm reservation at Gary Danko. We had heard good things but had never been, and at that point, we were simply grateful to have a table anywhere.

Despite traffic light outages causing us to arrive about 15 minutes late, the staff was incredibly accommodating. We were seated in a cozy loveseat booth, which turned out to be perfect. I don’t mind not sitting across from my spouse—especially since the five-course menu is very much a “choose your own adventure,” and we love sharing dishes. Sitting side by side made that easy and enjoyable, and the booth itself was very comfortable.

The food is classic French cooking done well. I especially loved the lobster dish offered as the second course. The oyster dish in the first course reminded me of The French Laundry’s Oysters and Pearls—not at the same level in terms of ingredients or presentation, but it reminded me of that classic dish and it was delightful.

My stuffed quail was a technically demanding dish, and it was executed beautifully. The quail was cooked just right—juicy, tender, and absolutely satisfying.

Our favorite course of the night ended up being the cheese course. If we were to do it again, we would opt for four courses and skip dessert altogether. The desserts were fine, but nothing particularly memorable or worth writing home about.

The wine pairing was reasonable and thoughtfully selected. At $170 for the tasting menu and $140 for the wine pairing, we felt the pricing was fair—especially for a restaurant that previously held a Michelin star.

That said, I can also see why Gary Danko eventually lost its star. The menu is very polished and technically sound, but it isn’t especially innovative. The service is excellent and delivers a classic, old-world level of refinement, though it may not resonate as strongly with a younger crowd. During our meal, most of the diners around us appeared to be 50+.

Overall, we still had a wonderful evening and were genuinely grateful to have such a solid meal to salvage our anniversary after our original restaurant canceled on us at the last minute.

text.text:
Cotogna is an absolute gem in San Francisco’s dining scene, a perfect balance of warmth, sophistication, and unforgettable flavor. Every dish we tried was a masterpiece, both in presentation and taste.

The prosciutto puff pastry was the perfect start, light, flaky, and beautifully balanced with the savory richness of the prosciutto. Then came the raviolo, which was pure heaven on a plate, delicate pasta filled with creamy perfection and that luscious egg yolk center that makes every bite feel indulgent.

The agnolotti pasta was another standout, with perfectly al dente pasta and a filling so flavorful and comforting it almost defies description. And the lamb pasta, wow. The depth of flavor, the tenderness of the lamb, and the subtle herbs made it one of the most memorable pasta dishes I’ve ever had.

Cotogna doesn’t just serve food, it creates an experience. The service was warm and professional, the atmosphere cozy yet refined, and every bite reminded us why this restaurant is so beloved.

If you love Italian cuisine done with heart, precision, and creativity, Cotogna is a must-visit.

editorialSummary.text: Bustling, bi-level bistro serving famous roast chicken & other seasonal wood-fired eats.

text.text:
Here's an honest review for Zuni, which had been on my list for over a year, but I tried my best not to get myself hyped up after reading some reviews beforehand.

Loved the vibe of the restaurant, and it was more spacious than we had expected. We were taken upstairs and around to a room that was tucked in, which we didn't even know existed. The interior is beautiful & inviting!

Service was highly attentive with just the right amount of politeness and formality. They refilled our glasses with water frequently and was quick to cater to our needs.

Now, these definitely matter, but I've mainly come to enjoy a good meal. A great meal if I'm splurging. Based on the reviews, we ordered:

• Shoestring potatoes: I personally didn't understand the hype. It's good, but it's really just thin fries at the end of the day, nothing mind-blowing.
• 18oz Ribeye: mid rare steak cooked to perfection, very creamy mashed potatoes, & roasted veggies were absolutely delicious. We were really glad to have tried this dish, which they had split it for us without having to ask.
• The famous roasted chicken: we ordered it pretty much as soon as we sat down, considering the 75min wait the menu & all reviews mentioned. Not sure if it's because we were already a bit full from other dishes we had while waiting, but it was quite underwhelming and disappointing to have waited so long and spend $75 on a dish that unfortunately did not seem anything special. The salad on top is fine (can't mess that up), but the chicken was actually little dry and the bread hadn't soaked up all the chicken flavor as expected. The value seemed questionable.
• Pavlova w/ citrus + pistachio dessert: my favorite order of the night! It was light, not too sweet, and loved the citrus + nutty pistachio combo. Absolutely recommend.

Overall, I appreciate the vibes, services, & even the dishes, although personally, I did find it a tad too salty (for all the dishes except for the dessert) for my tastebuds, but that could be subjective.

Fyi, 20% gratuity & 5% mandate is already included in the bill.

I recommend trying the place, but you can skip the chicken. RT Rotisserie or Bosque or Costco nearby can help satisfy that craving for better execution & cheaper value.

text.text:
Before anything else, visitors should know that Zuni automatically adds 25% on top of your bill — 20% tip plus an extra 5% for “healthcare.” As a European, I find this quite extreme. While a 20% tip can still be tolerated, being forced to pay an additional 5% to cover employee healthcare is something that should clearly be the employer’s responsibility, not the customer’s. This alone is a definite reason for a star deduction.

The restaurant itself is stylish, with attentive staff and a good atmosphere. The whole roasted chicken with salad is delicious and definitely the highlight. Just keep in mind that it takes about 1 to 1.5 hours to prepare, so it’s best to order it right away.

We also ordered the olives as an appetizer, but the portion consisted of extremely tiny olives — so small that they were difficult to pick up and felt more like leftover bits than a proper starter.

We then tried the Shoestring fries. Yes, they are labeled as “Shoestring fries,” but I genuinely didn’t expect them to be that thin. They were hard to pick up with a fork, awkward to eat with your hands, and overall not very satisfying.

The cocktails we had were fine, but nothing particularly memorable.

Overall: a nice restaurant with very good chicken and solid service, but the mandatory 25% surcharge and some disappointing sides — definitely impact the overall value and are the main reason I’m giving it 3 stars instead of 4.

text.text: Everyone raves about Zuni Cafe, and while it hit many high notes for me, there were one or two niggles that kept me from giving 5 stars. Obviously the star of the show was the roast chicken and salad, and it’s way more than two of us could eat. Great flavor and crispy skin, hit loads of high notes. We also got the little gems salad and the dressing was tangy and creamy, with the hazelnuts it was super balanced. Nice selection of wines by the glass. The cons? Our table was right on the window which meant we were super cold as there isn’t much insulation. And then because of being right on the window we were also directly facing another table that was almost on top of us. No real privacy. All around a great meal but would definitely find different seats next time.

text.text:
An absolute San Francisco gem! From the moment we walked in, the warm, bustling atmosphere made us feel like we were part of something special. The space has that perfect balance of sophisticated and comfortable.

The soup was outstanding – flavorful and perfectly seasoned. But the real star was the steak with mashed potatoes. The steak was cooked to perfection, with a beautiful sear and tender, juicy meat. And those mashed potatoes? Creamy, buttery heaven.

You can tell they source quality ingredients and know exactly what to do with them.

Zuni Café isn’t just a restaurant – it’s an institution, and for good reason.

The food quality is consistently excellent, and the lively yet intimate atmosphere makes every meal feel like an occasion.

Whether you’re a local or visiting San Francisco, this place is a must. Can’t wait to return!

People that just wanted to place an order. By the time they brought me my food it was cold. And when I asked if I could have it fresh the employee said she’d ask her manager. She came back 5 minutes later. And handed me the bag. I think they just slightly warmed it up my original food since it was lukewarm and did not taste good. The place also smelled really bad. A really sad representation of McDonald’s. I hope someone does something to make this location less disappointing.

text.text: This location is one block away from my apartment. The employees there have been really helpful to me during this past year.

text.text:
Unfortunately, I had a very negative experience at this McDonald’s.
The staff was consistently rude, and the manager demonstrated a complete lack of accountability. When I calmly explained that multiple employees were behaving disrespectfully—and that this behavior likely stems from leadership—the manager immediately denied any responsibility and showed no interest in addressing the issue.
Customer service starts at the top, and when leadership refuses to acknowledge problems, it’s no surprise the staff follows suit. I left feeling frustrated and unheard.
I would not recommend this location.

Overall this McDonalds need better employees or have them get trained on how to treat and deal with people

places.formattedAddress: 1581 Webster St #235, San Francisco, CA 94115, USA

editorialSummary.text: Contemporary spot for Japanese ramen (including hakata style), along with rice bowls, beer & sake.

text.text:
We arrived around 6:30pm on a Wednesday and was told we would be seated within 30 mins, which was to be expected during dinner time. The inside was packed with every table full, making it quite loud, but not too much that you couldn’t have a conversation with your table. Staff was quick and attentive.  Fun fact: you can ask for a bib so you don’t splash yourself when you eat.

The ramen broth was rich and flavorful. The egg was cooked pretty well too, the yolk was still runny and the whites weren’t too soft. The meat was tender, able to pull apart with the chopsticks.

The takoyaki was also very good and tasty as well. Not too soggy, but still crisp and creamy on the inside.

Overall this was a good experience and would come again, but perhaps a bit earlier to avoid the dinner rush.

text.text: They’ve expanded the restaurant and took over the space next door. Basically there’s no waiting like it used to be. We were there around 1:30 pm on a Monday and was immediately seated (for two)! No waiting at all. Service was friendly and very very quick! Probably about 4-5 minutes after our order we got our food which was deceptively served in small bowls. But there was a lot of food and noodles. The broth was sublime as expected, the noodles perfectly al dente (small thin) with really tender pieces of pork. It was delicious and service was excellent but it was sooo noisy inside? It was hard to talk but for this incredible ramen, it’s worth it!!

text.text:
I’ve heard about this place for a while and finally had the unexpected opportunity on Christmas Eve. A friend and I decided to meet at Japantown for dinner. We walked around and I remembered this place. We decided to eat here. We didn’t even have to wait 5 mins and we were called to our table! Our waitress was SO nice! She was above & beyond! Gave us recommendations and answered our questions; and staff was so attentive!
I ordered the vegan creamy broth ramen. It was good; however, I liked Hinodeya’s better as theirs was more creamy and I liked the taste better. Marufuku’s was still good though.
My friend had the Hankata Tonkatsu DX (The DX just means it has extra toppings). Apparently, this is one of the popular dishes. He said it was really good. We both finished our ramen so highly recommend.
We ordered the chicken Karaage as well. I have to say this was SO good!!! For those wondering, I know I ordered the vegan ramen but it’s because I don’t eat that much meat (obviously not 100% vegan or vegetarian).
Street parking can be tough but you if you’re lucky, you can find free residential parking or metered parking. Otherwise, there is an underground paid parking garage. If you take public transportation, the 38-Geary or 22-Fillmore (you’ll have to walk a few blocks, not bad as there’s plenty of shops & restaurants along the way).
I highly recommend this place!

text.text:
Trust Me: Join the WAITLIST on Google Maps before heading OVER!!!

Marufuku Ramen at the Japan Center is outstanding. The Japan Center itself feels like a small pocket of Japan, and walking in to a warm “irasshaimase!” instantly sets the tone. The ramen is perfectly cooked, deeply comforting, and genuinely transporting — one of those Ratatouille-level bites that takes you somewhere else. We waited almost 30 minutes to be seated and it was totally worth it — pro tip: check in or join the waitlist on Google Maps before heading over.. 🇯🇵🍜🍜🍜🍜🧾🧾🧾

Would highly recommend!!

editorialSummary.text: Neighborhood fixture serving traditional, old-school Italian entrees, steaks, and seafood.

text.text: 10/10 meal and experience. At a super busy time (6:45 ish) we were seated immediately at the counter, which I’d highly recommend. Gives you an appreciation for how hardworking and skilled the cooks are. The food was immaculate. We got shrimp cocktail, the Prime Rib (rivals House of Prime Rib), and the spaghetti with meatballs. Everything was delicious. It’s loud but the ambiance is part of the experience. Such a cool, classic old school dining experience.

text.text:
My wife and I love Original Joe’s in North Beach! It’s an absolute staple in the neighborhood and one of the most consistent restaurants in San Francisco.

The Food:

• Chicken Parm: I always order this. It’s a massive, thin cut of chicken with a generous portion of cheese and delicious marinara. Get the ravioli as your side!

• Seafood: My wife usually rotates between the Salmon with lemon beurre blanc or the Dungeness Crab Louie (they don’t skimp on the lump crab meat).

• Dessert: Do not leave without trying the Butter Cake. It is incredibly rich, so definitely share it.

The Wine (Pro Tip):

The wine list here is solid, but many of the bottles are heavily marked up. We used the Sip Savvy app to scan the menu for the best value vs rating bottle aligned with our palate. It flagged the Austin Hope 1L bottle as the Best Buy—it was $98, which Sip Savvy showed was one of the lowest markups on the menu for that quality. It paired perfectly with the Italian food. If you’re a wine person, definitely try the app here. It takes the guesswork out of finding great bottles without overpaying!

text.text: Original Joe's offers a truly classic San Francisco dining experience, the moment you step inside, the timeless atmosphere, complete with plush red booths and dark wood paneling, sets the perfect mood. The food quality is exceptional across the board: the grilled steak and rack of lamb were both cooked to perfection and beautifully presented, while the hearty Italian dishes, like the rich ravioli and comforting spaghetti with meat sauce, were satisfying classics. We started with delicious, crisp fried calamari and finished the night with a festive birthday dessert, capping off a meal where the service was consistently prompt, professional, and attentive. Original Joe's flawlessly delivers on high-quality ingredients, excellent service and a fantastic, old-school atmosphere.

text.text: Delicious old school North Beach restaurant with great service. The spicy rigatoni and grilled prawns were perfectly cooked and the sauce was delicious and mildly spicy. For dessert the warm butter cake with ice cream smelled amazing, with a hint of nostalgia. Take a look at the wall of family photos and celebrities. Reserve your table at least one month in advance, this place is always busy. There is a bar area for walk-ins, but people do get turned away on very busy nights.
